original_abstract: Ultrafine tuning of MOF structures at subangstrom or picometer levels can help improve separation selectivity for gases with subtle differences. However, for MOFs with a large enough pore size, the effect from ultrafine tuning on sorption can be muted. Here we show an integrative strategy that couples extreme pore compression with ultrafine pore tuning. This strategy is made possible by unique combination of two features of the partitioned acs (pacs) platform: multimodular framework and exceptional tolerance toward isoreticular replacement. Specifically, we use one module (ligand 1, L1) to shrink the pore size to an extreme minimum on pacs. A compression ratio of about 30% was achieved (based on the unit cell c/a ratio) from prototypical 1,4-benzenedicarboxylate-pacs to trans-1,3-cyclobutanedicarboxylate-pacs. This is followed by using another module (ligand 2, L2) for ultrafine pore tuning (<3% compression). This L1-L2 strategy increases the C2H2/CO2 selectivity from 2.6 to 20.8 and gives rise to an excellent experimental breakthrough performance. As the shortest cyclic dicarboxylate that mimics p-benzene-based moieties using a bioisosteric (BIS) strategy on pacs, trans-1,3-cyclobutanedicarboxylate offers new opportunities in MOF chemistry.
humanized_abstract: While ultrafine tuning the structure of MOFs at the subangstrom or even picometer level has the potential to enhance the selectivity between closely spaced gas components, the effect is often reduced as the impact of such fine adjustments to the structure is averaged out, particularly for MOFs with large enough pore diameters to accommodate the gas molecules. Here, we use an integrative strategy that compresses large pores to an extreme minimum by one of pore size and also fine-tunes their structure. Our ACS Materials Science & Engineering article entitled, Extreme pore compression of a MOF coupled with ultrafine structural adjustment shows that the unique features of the partitioned aromatic constraining scaffold (pacs) architecture (multimodular framework and its exceptional stability to isoreticular replacement) enabled using one ligand to achieve an extreme compression of the pore diameter by about 30% (based on the unit cell c/a ratio) from the prototypical 1,4-benzenedicarboxylate anchored on a pacs to that anchored on a trans-1,3-cyclobutanedicarboxylate pacs framework. This is followed by adjusting the structure further at the subangstrom or picometer level by using another ligand. This unique dual-ligand modularity enables our L1-L2 strategy to increase the selectivity between two gas molecules that have minor differences, here illustrated by a 8 times increase in the C2H2/CO2 adsorption selectivity compared to the 1,4-benzenedicarboxylate anchored pacs from 2.6 to 20.8 while achieving an excellent experimentally measured breakthrough uptake. Interestingly, using the shortest cyclic dicarboxylate (which mimics the bioisosteric, p-benzene based moieties) anchored on a pacs and the BIS design concept affords opportunities for the development of new MOF architectures.
